meta.title: Quiz 2 — DOM, Events & Async JavaScript
meta.description: DOM manipulation, event handling, async/await, promises, and browser APIs.

questions.questionMd: What is the primary purpose of the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)?

options.label: A
options.text: To sell hosting packages to web companies

options.label: B
options.text: To create new web browsers

options.label: C
options.text: To police the internet against malware

options.label: D
options.text: To develop protocols and guidelines that ensure long-term growth for the Web

questions.explanationMd: The W3C is the main international standards organization for the World Wide Web.

questions.questionMd: What is the primary purpose of the `fetch()` function in JavaScript?

options.label: A
options.text: To serialize JavaScript objects into JSON strings

options.label: B
options.text: To load resources asynchronously over a network using a Promise-based approach

options.label: C
options.text: To synchronously read files from the local file system

options.label: D
options.text: To parse HTML documents and manipulate the DOM

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, which company originally developed and used GraphQL before open-sourcing it?

options.label: A
options.text: Netflix

options.label: B
options.text: Facebook

options.label: C
options.text: GitHub

options.label: D
options.text: Spotify

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: How does the lecture describe XMLHttpRequest (XHR) compared to the Fetch API?

options.label: A
options.text: Simpler but less flexible

options.label: B
options.text: More secure and widely supported

options.label: C
options.text: Faster and more reliable

options.label: D
options.text: Clunky and harder to use

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: According to the course evaluation structure, what percentage of the final grade is determined by the midterm component?
questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Inside an ES6 class constructor, what does the `this` keyword refer to?

options.label: A
options.text: The global window object of the browser

options.label: B
options.text: The new object instance being created

options.label: C
options.text: The parent class from which the current class inherits

options.label: D
options.text: The HTML element that triggered the event

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Who created JSON according to the lecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Douglas Crockford

options.label: B
options.text: Ryan Dahl

options.label: C
options.text: Tim Berners-Lee

options.label: D
options.text: Brendan Eich

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: By default, without any additional options, which HTTP method does the `fetch()` API use when making a request?

options.label: A
options.text: PUT

options.label: B
options.text: POST

options.label: C
options.text: DELETE

options.label: D
options.text: GET

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: What is the primary purpose of the Fetch API in a web browser?

options.label: A
options.text: To manipulate the DOM after a page has loaded

options.label: B
options.text: To serialize JavaScript objects into strings

options.label: C
options.text: To load external resources such as text and JSON asynchronously

options.label: D
options.text: To define callback functions for user interface events

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Which set of programming languages is associated with front-end web development?

options.label: A
options.text: Node.js, PHP, and Go

options.label: B
options.text: SQL, XML, and SGML

options.label: C
options.text: Python, Java, and Ruby

options.label: D
options.text: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: In a Promise chain such as `fetch(url).then(a).then(b)`, what value does function `b` receive as its argument?

options.label: A
options.text: The original URL string passed to fetch()

options.label: B
options.text: The value returned by function a

options.label: C
options.text: The raw HTTP response object

options.label: D
options.text: The HTTP status code of the response

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly describes the old API that `fetch()` replaced for loading browser resources?

options.label: A
options.text: JSON.parse()

options.label: B
options.text: loadFromFile()

options.label: C
options.text: navigator.mediaDevices

options.label: D
options.text: XMLHttpRequest (XHR)

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following statements about `.then()` is correct according to the lecture?

options.label: A
options.text: It can only be called once per Promise object

options.label: B
options.text: It replaces the need for the fetch() function

options.label: C
options.text: It immediately executes the fetch request synchronously

options.label: D
options.text: It lets you attach functions to execute when a Promise succeeds or fails

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: In HTTP communication, what does a status code in the 200 range generally indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: The request was successful

options.label: B
options.text: The client must authenticate before proceeding

options.label: C
options.text: The server encountered an internal error

options.label: D
options.text: The resource was not found

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Why is storing data as JSON preferable to storing it as plain text when using `fetch()`?

options.label: A
options.text: JSON files load faster because they are compressed by the browser

options.label: B
options.text: JSON eliminates the need to manually parse field separators when deserializing structured data

options.label: C
options.text: Plain text requires XMLHttpRequest while JSON works with fetch

options.label: D
options.text: JSON is the only format the Fetch API is able to read

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: What does `response.text()` return?

options.label: A
options.text: A JSON object parsed from the response body

options.label: B
options.text: The raw bytes of the response body as a string immediately

options.label: C
options.text: A Promise that resolves with the response body as a string

options.label: D
options.text: The HTTP status code as a text string

questions.explanationMd: `response.text()` is an asynchronous method that returns a Promise, which resolves with the text representation of the response body.

questions.questionMd: When loading album data from a custom plain-text file, which of the following is a disadvantage mentioned in the lecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Numbers must be manually converted from strings using functions like parseInt()

options.label: B
options.text: The file cannot be loaded using the fetch() API

options.label: C
options.text: Plain-text files cannot be served over HTTP

options.label: D
options.text: Text files do not support arrays of items

questions.explanationMd: In plain-text files, everything is read as a string. Developers must manually parse numbers, dates, or other data types.

questions.questionMd: What does `response.status` contain when used inside a fetch success handler?

options.label: A
options.text: The HTTP status code of the request, such as 200

options.label: B
options.text: The text content of the fetched resource

options.label: C
options.text: A boolean indicating whether the request succeeded

options.label: D
options.text: The name of the file that was fetched

questions.explanationMd: `response.status` holds the numeric HTTP status code returned by the server (e.g., 200 for OK, 404 for Not Found).

questions.questionMd: What does `JSON.parse()` accept as its argument?

options.label: A
options.text: A JavaScript object to be serialized

options.label: B
options.text: A Promise returned by fetch()

options.label: C
options.text: A fetch response object

options.label: D
options.text: A JSON-formatted string

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.parse()` takes a JSON string and transforms it into a JavaScript object.

questions.questionMd: Compared to a custom plain-text format, what is a primary advantage of storing data in JSON format when using `fetch()`?

options.label: A
options.text: JavaScript has built-in support to automatically convert a JSON string into an object, eliminating manual parsing

options.label: B
options.text: JSON files are always smaller in size than plain-text files

options.label: C
options.text: JSON is the only format supported by the fetch() API

options.label: D
options.text: JSON files do not require a server to be accessed

questions.explanationMd: JSON is natively supported in JS via `JSON.parse()` and `response.json()`, making deserialization automatic and seamless.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly rewrites `fetch('f.txt').then(onResponse)` using an intermediate variable?

options.label: A
options.text: let p = onResponse; p = fetch('f.txt');

options.label: B
options.text: const p = fetch('f.txt'); p.resolve(onResponse);

options.label: C
options.text: const p = new Promise('f.txt'); p.then(onResponse);

options.label: D
options.text: const p = fetch('f.txt'); p.then(onResponse);

questions.explanationMd: `fetch` returns a Promise, which can be stored in a variable, and then chained with `.then()`.

questions.questionMd: In REST API terminology, what is the "base URL"?

options.label: A
options.text: The fallback URL used when the primary endpoint is unavailable

options.label: B
options.text: The root address of the API server to which all endpoint paths are appended

options.label: C
options.text: The full URL including all query parameters for a specific request

options.label: D
options.text: The URL of the web page that consumes the API

questions.explanationMd: The base URL acts as the consistent starting point for all endpoints in a REST API.

questions.questionMd: How do LANs and WANs relate to the concepts of Intranet and Internet respectively?

options.label: A
options.text: LANs form private internal networks (Intranet), while WANs connect these networks together to form the Internet

options.label: B
options.text: LANs use wireless connections only, while WANs use wired connections only

options.label: C
options.text: LANs are always public networks, while WANs are always private networks

options.label: D
options.text: LANs operate at the application layer, while WANs operate at the physical layer

questions.explanationMd: LANs are local, private networks (Intranets), and WANs connect them globally to form the broader Internet.

questions.questionMd: What does the `fetch()` function return?

options.label: A
options.text: A Promise

options.label: B
options.text: The raw text content of the requested resource

options.label: C
options.text: An XMLHttpRequest object

options.label: D
options.text: A JSON-parsed JavaScript object

questions.explanationMd: `fetch()` is asynchronous and immediately returns a Promise that resolves to the Response object.

questions.questionMd: What is the fundamental difference between a URL that points to a static file and a URL that represents a REST API endpoint?

options.label: A
options.text: A REST API endpoint returns HTML, while a static file URL always returns JSON

options.label: B
options.text: A REST API endpoint causes the server to dynamically generate a response, whereas a static file URL causes the server to return a stored file

options.label: C
options.text: A static file URL must include query parameters, while a REST API endpoint never does

options.label: D
options.text: A REST API endpoint always requires POST, while static file URLs always use GET

questions.explanationMd: Static files are just retrieved from the disk, while API endpoints execute logic dynamically on the server before responding.

questions.questionMd: Which statement correctly distinguishes the Internet from an Intranet?

options.label: A
options.text: The Internet uses IPv6 only, while an Intranet uses IPv4 only

options.label: B
options.text: The Internet is limited to universities, while an Intranet is open to the public

options.label: C
options.text: The Internet uses UDP, while an Intranet uses TCP exclusively

options.label: D
options.text: The Internet is the global interconnected network, while an Intranet is a private network for a limited group of users

questions.explanationMd: Intranets are private networks, typically used within an organization, whereas the Internet is global and public.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what does it mean to "deserialize" a string?

options.label: A
options.text: To split a string into an array using a separator

options.label: B
options.text: To turn a serialized string back into a JavaScript object

options.label: C
options.text: To encode a string using HTML entities

options.label: D
options.text: To convert a JavaScript object into a JSON string

questions.explanationMd: Deserialization is the process of converting serialized string data (like JSON) back into an in-memory data structure or object.

questions.questionMd: A Promise object can be in one of three states. Which of the following is NOT one of those states?

options.label: A
options.text: Fulfilled

options.label: B
options.text: Resolved

options.label: C
options.text: Pending

options.label: D
options.text: Rejected

questions.explanationMd: The three states of a Promise are Pending, Fulfilled, and Rejected. 'Resolved' describes the outcome, but the strict state name is Fulfilled.

questions.questionMd: When a method is used as a DOM event handler without binding, what does `this` refer to inside that method?

options.label: A
options.text: The global window object

options.label: B
options.text: The class instance that owns the method

options.label: C
options.text: The HTML element that the event handler was attached to

options.label: D
options.text: The event object passed to the handler

questions.explanationMd: In a standard event listener, `this` is automatically bound to the DOM element that fired the event unless bound explicitly or using an arrow function.

questions.questionMd: What is the key difference between `navigator.getUserMedia` and `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia`?

options.label: A
options.text: The deprecated version uses Promises; the newer version uses callbacks

options.label: B
options.text: Only the deprecated version supports video streaming

options.label: C
options.text: The newer version requires HTTPS while the deprecated version does not

options.label: D
options.text: The newer version returns a Promise, the deprecated version uses callbacks

questions.explanationMd: The modern `mediaDevices` API is Promise-based, whereas the old legacy API relied on success and error callback functions.

questions.questionMd: In the discography page example, how does the `SortButton` class communicate a sort action back to the `App` class?

options.label: A
options.text: It directly modifies the albumInfo property of the App instance

options.label: B
options.text: It dispatches a custom keyboard event that triggers the App's event listener

options.label: C
options.text: It fires a built-in DOM click event that the App class listens for on the document

options.label: D
options.text: It calls an onClickCallback function passed to it during construction, supplying the sort function as an argument

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: When defining methods inside an ES6 class body, which keyword must you omit?

options.label: A
options.text: class

options.label: B
options.text: function

options.label: C
options.text: const

options.label: D
options.text: return

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what is an important limitation of the CustomEvent API in JavaScript?

options.label: A
options.text: CustomEvents are not supported in any browser that uses the V8 engine

options.label: B
options.text: CustomEvents can only be dispatched and listened to on HTML elements, not on arbitrary class instances

options.label: C
options.text: CustomEvents can only be created inside ES6 class constructors

options.label: D
options.text: CustomEvents cannot carry any additional data in their event object

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: What problem does calling `this.methodName = this.methodName.bind(this)` in a constructor solve?

options.label: A
options.text: It prevents the method from being called more than once per event

options.label: B
options.text: It ensures this always refers to the class instance when the method is used as an event handler

options.label: C
options.text: It registers the method as a global function accessible from any scope

options.label: D
options.text: It copies the method's code into the HTML element for faster execution

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what does it mean to "serialize" an object?

options.label: A
options.text: To convert a string into a JavaScript object

options.label: B
options.text: To load an object from an external file using fetch

options.label: C
options.text: To turn an object into a string that can later be deserialized

options.label: D
options.text: To send an object directly to a server over HTTP

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: In a chained Promise pattern, what must `onResponse` return for the next `then()` in the chain to receive the text data?

options.label: A
options.text: A resolved string from response.status

options.label: B
options.text: The Promise returned by response.text()

options.label: C
options.text: The response object itself

options.label: D
options.text: A JSON object created with JSON.parse()

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following is a characteristic of first-class functions in JavaScript?

options.label: A
options.text: Functions must always be declared before the class definition

options.label: B
options.text: Functions cannot be stored in variables or object properties

options.label: C
options.text: Functions require the function keyword and cannot use arrow syntax

options.label: D
options.text: Functions can be passed as parameters to other functions

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: In the discography example, when a method is used as both a fetch callback and an event handler, what must be done in the constructor for `this` to work correctly in both cases?

options.label: A
options.text: Each method must be rebound to the instance using bind(this) and the result stored back on the object

options.label: B
options.text: The methods must be declared as arrow functions inside the constructor

options.label: C
options.text: Each method must be wrapped in a separate anonymous function before being passed as a callback

options.label: D
options.text: The methods must be made static so they do not rely on this

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: What is the correct syntax for using the real Fetch API with success and error handlers?

options.label: A
options.text: fetch('file.txt').catch(onSuccess).then(onError);

options.label: B
options.text: fetch('file.txt', onSuccess, onError);

options.label: C
options.text: fetch('file.txt').then(onSuccess, onError);

options.label: D
options.text: fetch('file.txt', { success: onSuccess, error: onError });

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Given the JSON object `{ "albums": [ { "name": "Daydream", "year": 1995 } ] }` parsed into a variable called `data`, how would you access the year of the first album?

options.label: A
options.text: data["year"]["albums"][0]

options.label: B
options.text: data[0].albums.year

options.label: C
options.text: data.albums[0].year

options.label: D
options.text: data.albums.year[0]

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: What is the role of SGML (Standard Generalized Markup Language) in relation to HTML?

options.label: A
options.text: SGML is a standard for specifying markup languages and was used by Tim Berners-Lee to create the HTML specification

options.label: B
options.text: SGML is a stylesheet language used to define the visual presentation of XML documents

options.label: C
options.text: SGML is a transport protocol that HTTP was built upon

options.label: D
options.text: SGML is a scripting language that adds interactivity to static HTML pages

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Which command is used to serve local files over HTTP so that `fetch()` can access them during development?

options.label: A
options.text: python3 -m http.server

options.label: B
options.text: python3 -m fetch server

options.label: C
options.text: npm start http

options.label: D
options.text: node server.js

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, why are web frameworks so commonly used in web development?

options.label: A
options.text: They make software architecture decisions ahead of time and provide starter code, reducing the number of decisions a developer must make

options.label: B
options.text: They automatically generate HTML and CSS from back-end database schemas

options.label: C
options.text: They compile JavaScript into machine code for faster execution in the browser

options.label: D
options.text: They replace the need to learn HTML, CSS, and JavaScript entirely

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Which method of a fetch `response` object provides built-in support for parsing JSON objects?

options.label: A
options.text: response.json()

options.label: B
options.text: response.parse()

options.label: C
options.text: response.text()

options.label: D
options.text: response.string()

questions.explanationMd: The `response.json()` method takes a Response stream and reads it to completion. It returns a promise that resolves with the result of parsing the body text as JSON.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following statements about `const` is correct according to the lecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Variables declared with `const` cannot be reassigned a new value.

options.label: B
options.text: `const` must be used for objects, while `let` is used for arrays.

options.label: C
options.text: `const` makes an object entirely immutable, preventing any property changes.

options.label: D
options.text: `const` variables are hoisted and accessible before declaration.

questions.explanationMd: `const` prevents reassignment of the variable identifier itself, though if it points to an object, properties of that object can still be modified.

questions.questionMd: The Internet protocol suite is often referred to by which acronym, and what do the letters stand for?

options.label: A
options.text: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)

options.label: B
options.text: UDP/IP (User Datagram Protocol / Internet Protocol)

options.label: C
options.text: TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)

options.label: D
options.text: WWW (World Wide Web)

questions.explanationMd: TCP/IP is the foundational suite of communication protocols used to interconnect network devices on the internet.

questions.questionMd: Why does the lecture recommend implementing the `Album` class before the `App` class?

options.label: A
options.text: Because `Album` class testing requires the front-end to be completed first

options.label: B
options.text: To avoid exceeding the maximum allowed memory size in the application

options.label: C
options.text: To prevent DOM manipulation from breaking the running backend services

options.label: D
options.text: Because the `App` class depends on the `Album` class, whereas the `Album` class has no dependencies.

questions.explanationMd: Building independent foundational models (`Album`) first ensures they are ready to be utilized by higher-level coordinating layers (`App`).

questions.questionMd: A `Promise` object in JavaScript is always in one of three states. What are they?

options.label: A
options.text: Waiting, Succeeded, Failed

options.label: B
options.text: Declared, Initialized, Resolved

options.label: C
options.text: Pending, Fulfilled, Rejected

options.label: D
options.text: Loading, Running, Finished

questions.explanationMd: These are the three official terminology states of a JavaScript Promise.

questions.questionMd: In the provided Node example, which module is imported to create a local web server handling HTTP?

options.label: A
options.text: http

options.label: B
options.text: fs

options.label: C
options.text: server

options.label: D
options.text: express

questions.explanationMd: Node.js has a built-in module called `http`, which allows Node.js to transfer data over the Hyper Text Transfer Protocol (HTTP).

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, the `fetch` API is made up of how many functions?

options.label: A
options.text: One

options.label: B
options.text: Two

options.label: C
options.text: Three

options.label: D
options.text: Many

questions.explanationMd: The core of the Fetch API relies on a single global function: `fetch()`. The rest of the API consists of objects like `Response`, `Request`, and `Headers`.

questions.questionMd: How is a static variable defined on a class in JS?

options.label: A
options.text: By using the `static` keyword before the variable name inside the class body

options.label: B
options.text: By declaring it outside of the class and referencing it inside

options.label: C
options.text: By prepending the `const` keyword before the property

options.label: D
options.text: By assigning it to `window` inside the constructor

questions.explanationMd: In modern ES6 JavaScript classes, the `static` keyword defines static methods or properties for a class.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly describes the old API that `fetch()` replaced?

options.label: A
options.text: JSON.parse()

options.label: B
options.text: jQuery.ajax()

options.label: C
options.text: XMLHttpRequest (XHR)

options.label: D
options.text: ActiveXObject

questions.explanationMd: Before `fetch()`, web developers primarily used the `XMLHttpRequest` object to request data from servers asynchronously.

questions.questionMd: Which HTTP method is considered "safe" by default?

options.label: A
options.text: PUT

options.label: B
options.text: POST

options.label: C
options.text: DELETE

options.label: D
options.text: GET

questions.explanationMd: An HTTP method is safe if it doesn't alter the state of the server. GET is used strictly to read data, making it safe.

questions.questionMd: In the provided fetch example, how is an image added to the web page after its URL is requested?

options.label: A
options.text: By using innerHTML to inject an tag directly

options.label: B
options.text: A CSS background image property is applied to an existing element

options.label: C
options.text: The URL is passed to a separate flash API to load the image data

options.label: D
options.text: A new element is created, its src property is set, and it is appended to the document body.

questions.explanationMd: The standard DOM manipulation way to add an image is using `document.createElement('img')`, assigning its `src`, and using `appendChild()`.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, a method is a function that belongs to what?

options.label: A
options.text: The global window object

options.label: B
options.text: The DOM, which optimizes the function for performance

options.label: C
options.text: An object or a class instance.

options.label: D
options.text: The fetch API block execution sequence

questions.explanationMd: A method is simply a function that is bound to an object as one of its properties.

questions.questionMd: When a method is used as a DOM event handler without binding, what does `this` refer to?

options.label: A
options.text: The global window object

options.label: B
options.text: The class instance that owns the method

options.label: C
options.text: The HTML element that the event handler was attached to.

options.label: D
options.text: The event object passed to the handler

questions.explanationMd: By default in the DOM, `this` inside an event listener callback refers to the element that triggered the event, not the enclosing class.

questions.questionMd: Why does the lecture recommend using arrow functions for callbacks instead of standard `function` expressions?

options.label: A
options.text: Arrow functions run faster in the V8 engine.

options.label: B
options.text: Arrow functions are the only way to return values.

options.label: C
options.text: They prevent the method from being called more than once per event.

options.label: D
options.text: Because arrow functions do not create their own `this` context, retaining the `this` value of the enclosing scope.

questions.explanationMd: Arrow functions use lexical scoping for `this`, making them ideal for callbacks inside classes where you want `this` to refer to the class instance.
